attributes.stem: A 15-year-old boy is brought in by his parents to see the General Practitioner due to pain in his left knee. He was playing basketball a week ago when he fell over and hit his knee hard on the ground. Since that time, the knee has not settled. He explains that in retrospect, he has had some slight knee pain over the last six weeks. He states the severity to currently be 5/10, however it is particularly bad at night which is resulting in poor sleep, inattention at school and poor mood. He has no significant medical history, takes no regular medications and is up to date with immunisations. Blood test results return with no abnormalities. An X-ray is taken (Image).
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This is a case of an osteosarcoma. Osteosarcomas are the most common malignant neoplasm of the bone in paediatric patients. They usually affect the metaphysis of long bones, particularly the femur, suggesting that there is a relationship between bone growth at the growth plate and lesion formation. They present with localised pain to the site of the tumour, often beginning insidiously over weeks to months. Pain is often described as being more prominent at night. Plain radiographic images show characteristic features of a sclerotic lesion, with a mix of radio-dense and radio-lucent areas that is often describes as a 'sunburst' appearance.

An osteoblastoma is a benign tumour of osteoid cells, presenting with chronic pain most commonly in the spine. Radiographically, the tumours rarely extend into soft tissue and are often small, round radiolucent lesions. This does not match the above description of the imaging.

Ewing's sarcoma is a malignant neoplasm of the bone and soft tissue presenting commonly in adolescents and young adults. It is the result of a genetic translocation of locus's on chromosome 11 and chromosome 22 which predispose the patient to the development of these tumours. These tumours are more commonly found in the diaphysis of bones and flat bones, rather than the metaphysis as is above. Similarly, the pattern on plain radiograph of Ewing's Sarcoma are lytic lesions that are multilaminar (onion-like).

Acute lymphoid leukaemia (ALL) is a malignant disease of the lymphoid progenitor cells that results in the overproduction of malignant, immature cells in the bone marrow. These cells are not functional, often spreading to the liver, spleen, lymph nodes and bone, resulting in organomegaly and crowding of the bone marrow. ALL typically presents with malaise, anorexia and bone pain as well as haematological evidence of bone marrow infiltration, including anaemia, leukopaenia, thrombocytopenia. This is not the case in this instance as this boy has localised pain and no signs of other features present on his blood tests.

Osteomyelitis is an infection of the bone. It is more likely to occur in those who have had trauma such as an open fracture, recent orthopaedic surgery, are immunosuppressed or a concurrent infection elsewhere in the body. Affected bone may separate from healthy bone causing the radiographic feature of sequestrum formation. They often present with pain at the sight, swelling, redness and fever. The time course does not match the above stem.
learningPoints: Osteosarcomas are the most common malignant neoplasm of long bones in the paediatric population.
references: WHO Classification of Tumours Editorial Board. Soft tissue and bone tumours: WHO classification of tumours. 5th ed. vol 3. Lyon, France: IARD Press; 2020

Papagelopoulos PJ, Galanis EC, Vlastou C, Nikiforidis PA, Vlamis JA, Boscainos PJ, et al. Current concepts in the evaluation and treatment of osteosarcoma. Orthopedics. 2000;23(8):858-67. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/10952054/
Angervall L, Enzinger FM. Extraskeletal neoplasm resembling Ewing's sarcoma. Cancer. 1975 Jul;36(1):240-51. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/1203852/
